He accumulated wealth through wise investments.

C1.definition: To publicly recommend or support
examples: Many experts advocate for renewable energy.

C1.definition: A claim that someone has done something wrong
examples: Serious allegations were made against the company.

C1.definition: To make suffering less severe
examples: The government took steps to alleviate poverty.
She tried to alleviate his concerns.

C1.definition: The quality of being open to more than one interpretation
examples: The contract was full of ambiguity.
There was no ambiguity in her response.

C1.definition: To regard as probable; expect or predict
examples: She anticipated the question and prepared an answer.
We anticipate strong demand for the product.

C1.definition: Logical and consistent
examples: The plan needs to be more coherent.
His argument was clear and coherent.

C1.definition: The act of obeying a rule or request
examples: Compliance with the law is mandatory.
She ensured compliance with safety regulations.
The company is in full compliance.

C1.definition: Including all or nearly all elements or aspects
examples: The exam was comprehensive and challenging.

C1.definition: To form or devise a plan or idea
examples: She conceived the idea during college.
It's hard to conceive of a world without technology.

C1.definition: To keep within certain limits
examples: She tried to confine her remarks to the topic.
The disease was confined to one region.
Don't confine yourself to one approach.

C1.definition: To make something physically stronger or more solid
examples: She consolidated her position as leader.
He needs to consolidate his skills.

C1.definition: The feeling that a person or thing is worthless
examples: She looked at him with contempt.
He showed contempt for the rules.

C1.definition: To deny the truth of a statement by asserting the opposite
examples: Don't contradict yourself in the interview.

C1.definition: To represent or show something in an image or story
examples: The painting depicts a rural landscape.
The novel depicts life in the 1920s.
She depicted the scene with great detail.

C1.definition: To become progressively worse
The quality of behaving in a careful and controlled way
examples: Use your own discretion in this matter.
She handled the situation with great discretion.
The judge used her discretion wisely.

C1.definition: A person's inherent qualities of mind and character
examples: His calm disposition makes him a good leader.
People with a positive disposition live longer.

C1.definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing
examples: Her silence was more eloquent than words.

C1.definition: To surround and have or hold within
examples: The course encompasses all major topics.

C1.definition: An attempt to achieve a goal
examples: Scientific endeavor has improved our lives.
She put great endeavor into her work.
Every creative endeavor starts with an idea.

C1.definition: To gradually destroy or be gradually destroyed
examples: Wind and rain erode the rocks.

C1.definition: To represent something as being larger or more important
examples: He tends to exaggerate his achievements.
She exaggerated the difficulty of the task.

C1.definition: A short extract from a film, broadcast, or text
examples: She read an excerpt from the novel.
Here is an excerpt from his speech.
The excerpt was taken from chapter three.

C1.definition: Relating to government revenue and taxation
examples: The government announced new fiscal policies.
The fiscal year ends in March.

C1.definition: To grow or develop in a healthy or vigorous way
examples: The business flourished under her leadership.
Creativity flourishes in a supportive environment.

C1.definition: Inspiring fear or respect through being impressively large or powerful
examples: The task seemed formidable at first.

C1.definition: Incapable of producing any useful result; pointless
examples: It's futile to argue with him.

C1.definition: About to happen
examples: She sensed that danger was imminent.

C1.definition: The conclusion that can be drawn from something
examples: What are the implications of this decision?

C1.definition: Without intention; accidentally
The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
examples: She is a person of great integrity.
He questioned the integrity of the research.

C1.definition: Producing a great deal of profit
examples: She found a lucrative career in finance.
Real estate can be very lucrative.

C1.definition: To express an idea clearly
examples: He is very articulate in meetings.
The report articulates the key findings.

C1.definition: Making something possible or likely
examples: A quiet environment is conducive to studying.
The conditions are conducive to growth.
Good leadership is conducive to success.

C1.definition: To reduce or restrict something
examples: They curtailed freedoms during the crisis.

C1.definition: To discourage someone from doing something
examples: Nothing could deter her from her goal.

C1.definition: A great difference
examples: There is a disparity in wages.
The disparity between rich and poor is growing.

C1.definition: To draw out a response or fact
examples: Her speech elicited a strong reaction.
The question elicited an honest answer.

C1.definition: To involve something as a necessary part
examples: The project entails a lot of research.

C1.definition: To encourage the development of something
examples: She fostered a love of reading.

C1.definition: To create difficulties for someone
examples: Bad weather hindered the rescue efforts.
Don't let fear hinder your growth.

C1.definition: Existing as a natural part of something
examples: There are inherent risks in every investment.
Creativity is inherent in human nature.

C1.definition: To make less severe or serious
examples: Steps were taken to mitigate the damage.

C1.definition: A subtle difference in meaning
examples: She understands the nuances of the language.
There are subtle nuances in his argument.

C1.definition: More important than anything else
Seeming reasonable or probable
Able to recover quickly from difficulties
Critical observation or examination
examples: The proposal came under close scrutiny.

C1.definition: Fully in agreement
examples: The jury reached a unanimous verdict.
There was unanimous support for the plan.

C1.definition: Liable to change rapidly and unpredictably
Absolutely necessary
examples: She has become indispensable to the team.

C1.definition: To go beyond the limits of something

C2.definition: To become less intense or widespread
examples: The storm showed no signs of abating.
The noise finally abated after midnight.

C2.definition: A departure from what is normal or expected
examples: The warm winter was an aberration.
This behavior is an aberration from his usual conduct.

C2.definition: Angry and bitter
examples: An acrimonious debate erupted in parliament.
She wanted to avoid an acrimonious discussion.

C2.definition: To combine or unite to form one structure
examples: The two companies amalgamated last year.
They decided to amalgamate their resources.

C2.definition: A thing belonging to a period other than that in which it exists
examples: The use of typewriters is an anachronism.
The monarchy is viewed by some as an anachronism.

C2.definition: Hostile and aggressive
examples: The belligerent nations refused to negotiate.

C2.definition: Concise and exact use of words
examples: Brevity is the soul of wit.
He was known for the brevity of his speeches.
The brevity of life makes every moment precious.

C2.definition: Given to sudden and unaccountable changes of mood
examples: She is known for her capricious behavior.

C2.definition: To find a way around an obstacle
examples: They tried to circumvent the regulations.
She circumvented the problem by using a different method.

C2.definition: Kept secret or done secretively
examples: They held clandestine meetings at night.

C2.definition: A confusing and difficult problem or question
examples: The budget deficit is a conundrum.
It's a moral conundrum with no easy answer.
She faced the conundrum of choosing between two jobs.

C2.definition: To make someone very weak
examples: The disease debilitated him for months.
Chronic stress can debilitate the immune system.

C2.definition: To describe or portray something precisely
examples: She delineated the boundaries of the project.
The report clearly delineates the risks involved.
It's important to delineate roles and responsibilities.

C2.definition: Cheerful and full of energy
examples: She was in an ebullient mood after the promotion.
His ebullient personality lights up every room.
The crowd was ebullient after the victory.

C2.definition: Lasting for a very short time
examples: Social media fame is often ephemeral.
The beauty of cherry blossoms is ephemeral.
Ephemeral trends come and go quickly.

C2.definition: Open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous
examples: The results of the study were equivocal.
She gave an equivocal answer to avoid commitment.

C2.definition: To make a problem or situation worse
examples: The drought exacerbated the food crisis.
His comments only exacerbated the tension.

C2.definition: Very attentive to detail and accuracy
examples: She is fastidious about her work.
He kept his house in fastidious order.
The editor was fastidious about grammar.

C2.definition: Fond of company; sociable
examples: He is naturally gregarious and makes friends easily.
Gregarious people thrive in social settings.

C2.definition: Relating to a distinctive individual characteristic
examples: She has an idiosyncratic sense of humor.
His writing style is highly idiosyncratic.
Each culture has its own idiosyncratic customs.

C2.definition: Not allowing fluid to pass through; unable to be affected
examples: The material is impervious to water.
He remained impervious to their pleas.

C2.definition: Proceeding in a gradual and harmful way
examples: Misinformation has an insidious effect on society.
He warned of the insidious influence of propaganda.

C2.definition: To place close together for contrasting effect
examples: The artist juxtaposed light and dark colors.
The documentary juxtaposes wealth and poverty.
She juxtaposed modern and traditional designs.

C2.definition: Very generous or forgiving
examples: He made a magnanimous gesture toward his rival.
A truly magnanimous leader inspires loyalty.

C2.definition: Typically of an action or activity, wicked or criminal
examples: His nefarious activities were finally exposed.

C2.definition: To make something unclear or unintelligible
examples: The jargon only serves to obfuscate the issue.
Don't try to obfuscate the facts.

C2.definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way
examples: The pernicious effects of pollution are well documented.
She warned of the pernicious influence of social media.

C2.definition: Representing the most perfect or typical example
examples: This is the quintessential British novel.
He embodies the quintessential spirit of entrepreneurship.

C2.definition: Having an obstinately uncooperative attitude
examples: The recalcitrant student refused to follow the rules.
They dealt with the recalcitrant nation firmly.
She was recalcitrant in the face of authority.

C2.definition: Kept secret, especially because it would not be approved
examples: She took a surreptitious glance at her phone.
He made surreptitious payments to the official.

C2.definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere
examples: Smartphones are ubiquitous in modern life.
Coffee shops have become ubiquitous in the city.
The brand has become ubiquitous worldwide.

C2.definition: Experienced in the imagination through the feelings of another
examples: She lived vicariously through her children.
He got vicarious pleasure from watching travel shows.

C2.definition: Occupying much space; very lengthy and detailed
examples: He produced voluminous notes during research.
The voluminous report covered every detail.

C2.definition: Having great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause
examples: She is a zealous advocate for education.
He was zealous in his efforts to reform the system.
The zealous fans cheered all night.

C2.definition: A trace of something that is disappearing or no longer exists
examples: Not a vestige of the old building remained.
The law is a vestige of a bygone era.
She found vestiges of the ancient civilization.

C2.definition: To accept something reluctantly but without protest
examples: She acquiesced to her parents' wishes.
He reluctantly acquiesced to the demand.
The committee acquiesced in the decision.

C2.definition: A person or thing that is the direct opposite
examples: She is the antithesis of her sister.
War is the antithesis of peace.
His behavior was the antithesis of professionalism.

C2.definition: To find out something for certain
examples: Police are trying to ascertain the facts.
She ascertained the cause of the problem.
It's difficult to ascertain the truth.

C2.definition: Standing out so as to be clearly visible
examples: She was conspicuous by her absence.
The error was conspicuous to everyone.
He made a conspicuous effort to apologize.

C2.definition: Causing harm or damage
examples: Smoking has deleterious effects on health.
Pollution has a deleterious impact on wildlife.

C2.definition: Outstandingly bad; shocking
examples: It was an egregious error of judgment.

C2.definition: To make something clear; to explain
examples: Further research is needed to elucidate the mechanism.

C2.definition: Unreasonably high in price
examples: She refused to pay the exorbitant fee.
Exorbitant rents are driving people away.

C2.definition: Treating serious issues with inappropriate humor
examples: He was being facetious, not serious.
She made a facetious remark during the meeting.
Try not to be facetious about this.

C2.definition: Acting quickly without thought
examples: Her impetuous nature often got her into trouble.
Don't be so impetuous; think before you act.

C2.definition: Impossible to stop or prevent
examples: She was inexorable in her pursuit of justice.

C2.definition: Showing great attention to detail
examples: She is meticulous in her work.
He kept meticulous records of every transaction.

C2.definition: Designed to impress or attract notice
examples: She wore an ostentatious diamond necklace.
The party was ostentatious and extravagant.

C2.definition: To prevent from happening
examples: Bad weather precluded any outdoor activity.
His age precludes him from joining the army.

C2.definition: A natural tendency to behave in a particular way
examples: She has a propensity for exaggeration.
He showed a propensity for violence.
Humans have a propensity for curiosity.

C2.definition: To refuse to accept or be associated with
examples: He repudiated all responsibility for the error.

C2.definition: Equivalent in seriousness to; virtually the same as
examples: His silence was tantamount to an admission of guilt.
The offer is tantamount to an insult.
Ignoring the problem is tantamount to accepting it.

C2.definition: Leaving no doubt; unambiguous
examples: He made an unequivocal statement of support.

C2.definition: Clear, logical, and convincing
examples: His reasoning was cogent and well-structured.

C2.definition: To take the place of something
examples: She was supplanted by a younger rival.
